title: Satistics Problem Generator
text:
This an ongoing project to create a series of randomly generate problems and their fully worked solutions for use in an undergraduate psychology course.

1 About the Project (draft)
The first version was developed and deployed using google colab. The goal was to write functions that could be used to endlessly generate new practice problems and their solutions. It was written…
The original version was not as user-friendly as intended and did not meet digital accessibility guidelines so it pivoted to static document generation.
… examples hosted here document and display the functionality of the second version. Once this page is mostly documented and ready, a significant rewrite and restructuring of the codebase will begin. The rewrite aims to improve the code itself and prepare it for new features, more flexibility, and improved functionality.


2 information below is old will be updated soon


3 Documents to produce accessible .html practice problems
Each document will produces 30 practice problems in a .html slideshow rendered with revealjs. Each slide is scrollable and contains a single problem. Problems are generated with a randomly determined sample size between 5 and 15 per group. When more than one group, sample size is equivalent between groups.

08 - Hypothesis_Test_z-Scores.qmd: hypothesis testing with z-scores
09 - Single-Sample_t-Tests.qmd: one-sample t-tests
10 - Independent-Samples_t-Tests.qmd: independent-samples t-tests
11 - Dependent-Samples_t-Tests.qmd: dependent-samples t-tests
12 - one-way_ANOVA.qmd: one-way ANOVA
13 - repeated-measures_ANOVA: repeated-measures ANOVA
14 - factorial_ANOVA: two-factor ANOVA



4 Title II Updates in the immediate queue

One-Way ANOVA
Repeated-Measures ANOVA



5 Title II Updates remaining

standard deviation exercises
basic z-scores and using z-scores with sample means



6 Functionality to add

correlation
regression
two-factor ANOVA
Post-hoc testing for ANOVA
Alt-text for figures. Can the alt- and descriptive-text be auto generated based on the matplotlib input?
include specifying the hypotheses for NHST (null and alternative)



7 Future directions

seperate defining question from displaying the results

consider adding: class Question: that will hold the question (this might not be necessary)
consider adding: class Assignment: to hold a collection of question objects.

will output a formatted assignment and it’s answer key


add functionality to specify some details when creating the question such as the distribution

add common standardized distributions as options

expand the text of the question, inlcude a list of common variables
switch from slide-based format to a book

combine all into a single book style document.
add instructions and a full walkthrough for each problem type

title: 2  Hypothesis Testing with z-Scores
text:
3 About
the one-sample z-Test problems use the RamdomData class which requires:

the groups variable set to 1: groups = 1
an intger for the sample size (e.g., n = 30)
a call to the z_test() method

In these sample problems, the per-group sample size is randomly set between 5 and 15. An example funciton call is included below.

sample_size = random.randint(5,15)
RandomData(groups = 1, n = sample_size).z_test()

The necessary summary statistics for these data \[M_A = {27.54}\] \[{\sigma} = {6}\] \[n = {13}\]
State the Hypotheses  \[H_0: \mu = 25\] \[H_1: \mu \ne 25\] 
The decision criteria:  \(z_{crit} = \pm{2.58}, \alpha_{two-tailed} = {0.01}\) Calculate the standard error  \[\sigma_M = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}\]  \[\sigma_M = \frac{6}{\sqrt{13}}\]  \[\sigma_M = \frac{6}{3.61}\]  \[\sigma_M = {1.66}\]  Calculate \(z_{obt}\)  \[z_{obt} = {\frac{M - \mu}{\sigma_M}}\]  \[z_{obt} = \frac{27.54 - 25}{1.66}\]  \[z_{obt} = \frac{2.54}{1.66}\]  \[z_{obt} = {1.53}\]  Calculate Cohen’s d for effect size  \[d = \frac{M - \mu}{\sigma}\]  \[d = \frac{27.54 - 25}{6}\]  \[d = \frac{2.54}{6}\]  \[d = {0.42}\]  The results:  fail to reject the null hypothesis, results not significant,  z = 1.53, p > 0.01, d = 0.42

title: 3  One-Sample t-tests
text:
4 About
the one-sample t-Test problems use the RamdomData class which requires:

the groups variable set to 1: groups = 1
an intger for the sample size (e.g., n = 30)
a call to the one_sample_t_test() method

In these sample problems, the per-group sample size is randomly set between 5 and 15. An example funciton call is included below

sample_size = random.randint(5,15)
RandomData(groups = 1, n = sample_size).one_sample_t_test()



5 Problem 1
sample_size = random.randint(5,15)
RandomData(groups = 1, n = sample_size).one_sample_t_test()

 The necessary summary statistics for these data \[M_A = {19.12}\] \[s^2 = {0.41}\] \[n = {8}\]
State the Hypotheses  \[H_0: \mu = 13\] \[H_1: \mu \ne 13\] 
The decision criteria:  \(t_{crit} = \pm{2.36}, \alpha_{two-tailed} = {0.05}, df = {7}\) Calculate the standard error  \[s_M = \sqrt{\frac{s^2}{n}}\]  \[s_M = \sqrt{\frac{0.41}{8}}\]  \[s_M = \sqrt{0.05}\]  \[s_M = {0.22}\]  calculate \(t_{obt}\)  \[t_{obt} = {\frac{M - \mu}{s_M}}\]  \[t_{obt} = \frac{19.12 - 13}{0.22}\]  \[t_{obt} = \frac{6.12}{0.22}\]  \[t_{obt} = {27.82}\]  calculate Cohen’s d  \[d = \frac{M - \mu}{s}\]  \[d = \frac{19.12 - 13}{0.64}\]  \[d = \frac{6.12}{0.64}\]  \[d = {9.56}\] 
The results:  reject the null hypothesis, results are significant,  t(7) = 27.82, p < 0.05, d = 9.56

title: 4  Independent-Samples t-tests
text:
5 About
the independent-samples t-Test problems use the RamdomData class which requires:

the groups variable set to 2: groups = 2
the sample size per group - all groups will have the same sample size
a call to the independent_samples_t_test() method

In these sample problems, the per-group sample size is randomly set between 5 and 15. An example funciton call is included below

sample_size = random.randint(5,15)
RandomData(groups = 2, n = sample_size).independent_samples_t_test()



6 Problem 1
Given the following between-subjects data, is the mean of \(Group_A\) significantly different from the mean of \(Group_B\)? Use a \({2}\) tailed-test with \(\alpha = {0.05}\)

 The necessary summary Statistis for these data: \[M_A = {22.33}, M_B = {17.58}\] \[SS_A = {278.67}, SS_B = {176.92}\] \[n_A = {12}, n_B = {12}\]
State the Hypotheses  \[H_0: \mu_A - \mu_B = 0\] \[H_1: \mu_A - \mu_B \ne 0\] 
The decision criteria:  \(t_{crit} = \pm{2.07}, \alpha_{two-tailed} = {0.05}, df = {22}\) Calculate the pooled variance:  \[s_p^2 = {\frac{SS_A + SS_B}{df_A + df_B}}\]  \[s_p^2 = {\frac{278.67 + 176.92}{11 + 11}}\]  \[s_p^2 = \frac{455.59}{22}\]  \[s_p^2 = {20.71}\] 
Calculate the estimated error of the difference between means: \(s_{(M_A - M_B)}\)  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{\frac{s_p^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_p^2}{n_1}}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{\frac{20.71}{12} + \frac{20.71}{12}}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{1.73 + 1.73}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{3.46}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = {1.86}\] 
Calculate \(t_{obt}\)  \[t_{obt} = {\frac{(M_A - M_B) - (\mu_A - \mu_B)}{s_{(M_A - M_B)}}}\]  \[t_{obt} = \frac{(22.33 - 17.58) - {0}}{1.86}\]  \[t_{obt} = \frac{4.75}{1.86}\]  \[t_{obt} = {2.55}\] 
Calculate Cohen’s d  \[d = \frac{M_A - M_B}{\sqrt{s_p^2}}\]  \[d = \frac{(22.33 - 17.58)}{{{\sqrt{20.71}}}}\]  \[d = \frac{(4.75)}{4.55}\]  \[d = {1.04}\] 
The results:  reject the null hypothesis, results are significant,  t(22) = 2.55, p < 0.05, d = 1.04

 The necessary summary Statistis for these data: \[M_A = {10.67}, M_B = {14.78}\] \[SS_A = {36.0}, SS_B = {29.56}\] \[n_A = {9}, n_B = {9}\]
State the Hypotheses  \[H_0: \mu_A - \mu_B = 0\] \[H_1: \mu_A - \mu_B \ne 0\] 
The decision criteria:  \(t_{crit} = \pm{2.12}, \alpha_{two-tailed} = {0.05}, df = {16}\) Calculate the pooled variance:  \[s_p^2 = {\frac{SS_A + SS_B}{df_A + df_B}}\]  \[s_p^2 = {\frac{36.0 + 29.56}{8 + 8}}\]  \[s_p^2 = \frac{65.56}{16}\]  \[s_p^2 = {4.1}\] 
Calculate the estimated error of the difference between means: \(s_{(M_A - M_B)}\)  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{\frac{s_p^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_p^2}{n_1}}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{\frac{4.1}{9} + \frac{4.1}{9}}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{0.46 + 0.46}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = \sqrt{0.92}\]  \[s_{(M_A - M_B)} = {0.96}\] 
Calculate \(t_{obt}\)  \[t_{obt} = {\frac{(M_A - M_B) - (\mu_A - \mu_B)}{s_{(M_A - M_B)}}}\]  \[t_{obt} = \frac{(10.67 - 14.78) - {0}}{0.96}\]  \[t_{obt} = \frac{-4.11}{0.96}\]  \[t_{obt} = {-4.28}\] 
Calculate Cohen’s d  \[d = \frac{M_A - M_B}{\sqrt{s_p^2}}\]  \[d = \frac{(10.67 - 14.78)}{{{\sqrt{4.1}}}}\]  \[d = \frac{(-4.11)}{2.02}\]  \[d = {-2.03}\] 
The results:  reject the null hypothesis, results are significant,  t(16) = -4.28, p < 0.05, d = -2.03

title: 6  One-Way ANOVA
text:
7 About
the one-way ANOVA problems use the RamdomData class which requires:

an integer value for the number of groups
the sample size per group - all groups will have the same sample size
a call to the anova() method with test = "one-way"

Each of the problems below sets factor levels using a random integer between 2 and 5. Per-group sample size is randomly set between 5 and 15. An example funciton call is included below

n_groups = random.randint(2,5)
sample_size = random.randint(5,15)
RandomData(groups = n_groups, n = sample_size).anova(test = "one-way")

Summary statistics for these data: \(G = {1290} \quad \Sigma{X^2} = {47998} \quad k = {4} \quad N = {36}\) 
\(T_{A} = {293} \quad SS_{A} = {126.22}\)  \(T_{B} = {293} \quad SS_{B} = {540.22}\)  \(T_{C} = {371} \quad SS_{C} = {491.56}\)  \(T_{D} = {333} \quad SS_{D} = {148.0}\)   State the Hypotheses  \(H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C = \mu_D\)  \(H_1:\) At least one mean is different   The decision criteria:  \(F_{crit} = {4.46}, \alpha = {0.01}\) 
Calculate the Degrees of Freedom  \[df_{total} = N - 1\]  \[df_{total} = {36} - 1\]  \[df_{total} = {35}\]  \[df_{between} = k - 1\]  \[df_{between} = {4} - 1\]  \[df_{between} = {3}\]  \[df_{within} = N - K\]  \[df_{within} = {36} - {4}\]  \[df_{within} = {32}\] 
Calculate the Sum of Squares  \[SS_{total} = \Sigma X^2 - \frac{G^2}{N}\]  \[SS_{total} = {47998} - \frac{1290^2}{36}\]  \[SS_{total} = {47998} - \frac{1664100}{36}\]  \[SS_{total} = {47998} - {46225.0}\]  \[SS_{total} = {1773.0}\]  \[SS_{within} = \Sigma SS_{inside\_each\_condition}\]  \[SS_{within} = {126.22 + 540.22 + 491.56 + 148.0}\]  \[SS_{within} = {1306.0}\]  \[SS_{between} = SS_{total} - SS_{within}\]  \[SS_{between} = {1773.0} - {1306.0}\]  \[SS_{between} = {467.0}\]  note: the other way to calculate \(SS_{betwen}\) is:  \[SS_{between} = \Sigma{\frac{T^2}{n}} - \frac{G^2}{N}\] 
Calculate the Mean Squares  \[MS_{between} = \frac{SS_{between}}{df_{between}}\]  \[MS_{between} = \frac{467.0}{3}\]  \[MS_{between} = {155.67}\] 
\[MS_{within} = \frac{SS_{within}}{df_{within}}\]  \[MS_{within} = \frac{1306.0}{32}\]  \[MS_{within} = {40.81}\] 
Calculate the F-Ratio  \[F_{obt} = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}\]  \[F_{obt} = \frac{155.67}{40.81}\]  \[F_{obt} = {3.81}\] 
Calculate \(\eta^2\)  \[\eta^2 = \frac{SS{between}}{SS_{total}}\]  \[\eta^2 = \frac{467.0}{1773.0}\]  \[\eta^2 = {0.26}\] 
The results:  fail to reject the null hypothesis, results not significant,  \(F({3}, {32}) = {3.81}, p > {0.01}, \eta^2 = {0.26}\)

\[MS_{within} = \frac{SS_{within}}{df_{within}}\]  \[MS_{within} = \frac{245.51}{33}\]  \[MS_{within} = {7.44}\]

title: 7  Repeated-Measures ANOVA

8 About
the repeated-measures ANOVA problems use the RamdomData class which requires:

an integer value for the number of groups
the sample size per group - all groups will have the same sample size
a call to the anova() method with test = "repeated-measures"

Each of the problems below sets factor levels using a random integer between 2 and 5. Per-group sample size is randomly set between 4 and 10. An example funciton call is included below

n_groups = random.randint(2,5)
sample_size = random.randint(4,10)
RandomData(groups = n_groups, n = sample_size).anova(test = "repeated-measures")

title: 8  Two-Factor ANOVA
text:
9 About
the factorial ANOVA problems use the FactorialData class which requires:

a tuple to specify the levels of each factor - (factor A, factor B)
the sample size per group - all groups will have the same sample size

Each of the problems below sets factor levels randomly with 2 or 3 levels. Per-group sample size is randomly set between 5 and 15. An example funciton call is included below

levels_factor_a = random.randint(2,3)
levels_factor_b = random.randint(2,3)
n = random.randint(5,15)
FactorialData(design = (levels_factor_a, levels_factor_b), group_n = n).factorial_ANOVA()



10 Problem 1
Given the following data, use a use a 2-Factor ANOVA with \(\alpha = {0.01}\) to analyze the following data Full Group Summary Data  \(T_{A1B1} = {319} \quad M_{A1B1} = {29.0}\quad SS_{A1B1} = {52.0} \quad n_{A1B1} = {11}\)  \(T_{A1B2} = {238} \quad M_{A1B2} = {21.64}\quad SS_{A1B2} = {18.55} \quad n_{A1B2} = {11}\)  \(T_{A2B1} = {226} \quad M_{A2B1} = {20.55}\quad SS_{A2B1} = {54.73} \quad n_{A2B1} = {11}\)  \(T_{A2B2} = {304} \quad M_{A2B2} = {27.64}\quad SS_{A2B2} = {44.55} \quad n_{A2B2} = {11}\)  \(T_{A3B1} = {227} \quad M_{A3B1} = {20.64}\quad SS_{A3B1} = {68.55} \quad n_{A3B1} = {11}\)  \(T_{A3B2} = {278} \quad M_{A3B2} = {25.27}\quad SS_{A3B2} = {84.18} \quad n_{A3B2} = {11}\)  \(\Sigma X^2 = {39478}\)  \(G = {1592}\)  Stage 1 ANOVA Calculations  Calculate the Degrees of Freedom: \(df_{total}, df_{within}, \text{ and } df_{between}\)  \(df_{total} = N - 1\)  \(df_{total} = {66} - 1\)  \(df_{total} = {65}\)  \(df_{between} = k - 1\)  \(df_{between} = {6} - 1\)  \(df_{between} = {5}\)  \(df_{within} = N - K\)  \(df_{within} = {66} - {6}\)  \(df_{within} = {60}\) 
Calculate the Sum of Squares: \(SS_{total}, SS_{within}, \text{ and } SS_{between}\)  \(SS_{total} = \Sigma X^2 - \frac{G^2}{N}\)  \(SS_{total} = {39478} - \frac{1592^2}{66}\)  \(SS_{total} = {39478} - \frac{2534464}{66}\)  \(SS_{total} = {39478} - {38400.97}\)  \(SS_{total} = {1077.03}\)  \(SS_{within} = \Sigma SS_{inside\_each\_condition}\)  \(SS_{within} = {52.0 + 18.55 + 54.73 + 44.55 + 68.55 + 84.18}\)  \(SS_{within} = {322.56}\)  \(SS_{between} = SS_{total} - SS_{within}\)  \(SS_{between} = {1077.03} - {322.56}\)  \(SS_{between} = {754.47}\)  note: the other way to calculate \(SS_{betwen}\) is:  \(SS_{between} = \Sigma{\frac{T^2}{n}} - \frac{G^2}{N}\) 
 Stage 2 calculations for \(Factor_A\) Summary Data for Factor_A  \(T_{A_1} = {557}  \quad n_{A_1} = {22}\)  \(T_{A_2} = {530}  \quad n_{A_2} = {22}\)  \(T_{A_3} = {505}  \quad n_{A_3} = {22}\)   State the Hypotheses  \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3\)  \(H_1:\) At least one mean is different  partition df for \(Factor_A\)  \(df_{Factor_A} = k_{Factor_A} - 1\)  \(df_{Factor_A} = {3} - 1\)  \(df_{Factor_A} = {2}\)   The decision criteria:  \(F_{crit} = {4.98}, \alpha = {0.01}\)  Partition the SS for \(Factor_A\) Calculate \(SS_{Factor_A}\)  \(\Sigma{\frac{{T_{Factor_A}}^2}{n_{Factor_A}}} - \frac{G^2}{N}\)  \(SS_{Factor_A} = {\frac{{557}^2}{22} + \frac{{530}^2}{22} + \frac{{505}^2}{22}} - \frac{{1592}^2}{66}\)  \(SS_{Factor_A} = {\frac{{310249}}{22} + \frac{{280900}}{22} + \frac{{255025}}{22}} - \frac{{2534464}}{66}\) \(SS_{Factor_A} = {{14102.23} + {12768.18} + {11592.05}} - {38400.97}\)  \(SS_{Factor_A} = {38462.46} - {38400.97}\)  \(SS_{Factor_A} = {61.49}\)  Mean Squares for \({Factor_A}\)  \(MS_{Factor_A} = \frac{SS_{Factor_A}}{df_{Factor_A}}\)  \(MS_{Factor_A} = \frac{61.49}{2}\)  \(MS_{Factor_A} = {30.74}\)
Calculate the F-Ratio for \({Factor_A}\)  \(F_{Factor_A} = \frac{MS_{Factor_A}}{MS_{Within}}\)  \(F_{Factor_A} = \frac{30.74}{5.38}\)  \(F_{Factor_A} = {5.71}\) 
 Calculate \(\eta_p^2 \text{ for } Factor_A\)  \(\eta_p^2 = \frac{{SS_{{Factor_A}}}}{{SS_{{Total}} - SS_{{Factor_B}} - SS_{{AxB}}}}\) \(\eta_p^2 = \frac{61.49}{{1077.03} - {34.91} - {658.07}}\) \(\eta_p^2 = \frac{61.49}{384.05}\) \(\eta_p^2 = {0.16}\) The results:  reject the null hypothesis, results are significant,  \(F({2}, {60}) = {5.71}, p < {0.01}, \eta_p^2 = {0.16}\)   Stage 2 calculations for \(Factor_B\) Summary Data for Factor_B  \(T_{B_1} = {772}  \quad n_{B_1} = {33}\)  \(T_{B_2} = {820}  \quad n_{B_2} = {33}\)   State the Hypotheses  \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2\)  \(H_1: \mu_1 \ne \mu_2\)  partition df for \(Factor_B\)  \(df_{Factor_B} = k_{Factor_B} - 1\)  \(df_{Factor_B} = {2} - 1\)  \(df_{Factor_B} = {1}\)   The decision criteria:  \(F_{crit} = {7.08}, \alpha = {0.01}\)  Partition the SS for \(Factor_B\) Calculate \(SS_{Factor_B}\)  \(\Sigma{\frac{{T_{Factor_B}}^2}{n_{Factor_B}}} - \frac{G^2}{N}\)  \(SS_{Factor_B} = {\frac{{772}^2}{33} + \frac{{820}^2}{33}} - \frac{{1592}^2}{66}\)  \(SS_{Factor_B} = {\frac{{595984}}{33} + \frac{{672400}}{33}} - \frac{{2534464}}{66}\) \(SS_{Factor_B} = {{18060.12} + {20375.76}} - {38400.97}\)  \(SS_{Factor_B} = {38435.88} - {38400.97}\)  \(SS_{Factor_B} = {34.91}\)  Mean Squares for \({Factor_B}\)  \(MS_{Factor_B} = \frac{SS_{Factor_B}}{df_{Factor_B}}\)  \(MS_{Factor_B} = \frac{34.91}{1}\)  \(MS_{Factor_B} = {34.91}\)
Calculate the F-Ratio for \({Factor_B}\)  \(F_{Factor_B} = \frac{MS_{Factor_B}}{MS_{Within}}\)  \(F_{Factor_B} = \frac{34.91}{5.38}\)  \(F_{Factor_B} = {6.49}\) 
 Calculate \(\eta_p^2 \text{ for } Factor_B\)  \(\eta_p^2 = \frac{{SS_{{Factor_B}}}}{{SS_{{Total}} - SS_{{Factor_A}} - SS_{{AxB}}}}\) \(\eta_p^2 = \frac{34.91}{{1077.03} - {61.49} - {658.07}}\) \(\eta_p^2 = \frac{34.91}{357.47}\) \(\eta_p^2 = {0.1}\) The results:  fail to reject the null hypothesis, results not significant,  \(F({1}, {60}) = {6.49}, p > {0.01}, \eta_p^2 = {0.1}\)   Stage 2 calculations for the \(AxB\) interaction   State the Hypotheses  \(H_0:\) There is no AxB interation  \(H_1:\) There is an AxB interaction  Partition df for the interaction  \(df_{AxB} = df_{Between} -df_{Factor_A} - df_{Factor_B}\)  \(df_{AxB} = {5} - {2} - {1}\)  \(df_{AxB} = {2}\) 
 The decision criteria:  \(F_{crit} = {4.98}, \alpha = {0.01}\)  Partition the SS for \(AxB\) Calculate \(SS_{AxB}\)  \(SS_{{AxB}} = SS_{{Between}} - SS_{{Factor_A}} - SS_{{Factor_B}}\) \(SS_{AxB} = {754.47} - {61.49} - {34.91}\)  \(SS_{AxB} = {658.07}\)  Mean Squares for \({AxB}\)  \(MS_{AxB} = \frac{SS_{AxB}}{df_{AxB}}\)  \(MS_{AxB} = \frac{658.07}{2}\)  \(MS_{AxB} = {329.04}\)
Calculate the F-Ratio for \({AxB}\)  \(F_{AxB} = \frac{MS_{AxB}}{MS_{Within}}\)  \(F_{AxB} = \frac{329.04}{5.38}\)  \(F_{AxB} = {61.16}\)
